description: A large natural elevation of earth's surface rising dramatically above surrounding land. 'Tomora' names the great risings of the earth—ancient stone reaching toward sky, holding snow and source-waters, standing as witnesses across generations.
sound_symbolism: The word rises from earth to sky. 'To' begins with dental grounding, the mountain's foundation. 'Mo' gathers its massive, enduring body, and 'ra' lifts and opens at the summit where stone meets sky.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun. Plural with 'lo': 'lo tomora' (mountains). A mountain is a landform rather than a detached or crafted object, so it has no canonical classifier. Distinguished from 'molira' (hill) by scale and permanence. Often sacred in traditional contexts. Used metaphorically for obstacles, aspirations, and enduring presence.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Mountains teach aspiration and perspective—the climb reveals new vistas, and from the summit one sees the interconnection of all below.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Mountains were sacred in nearly all traditional cultures—homes of spirits, sources of waters, places where earth touches sky.
